title: The Macro Minute: What do latest the key high-frequency economic data signal about global economies?

Happy Wednesday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Delt, to
present our macro minute for Wednesday,
December 3rd, 2025. As always, we'll
start with the executive summary from
today's lead off morning note. So, let's
hop right in. Today's key macro question
is, what do the latest key high
frequency economic data signal about the
health of the US and global economies?
The short answer is the US data were
generally supported our U-shaped economy
thesis and suggest the Fed is falling
further behind the rates curve. The
global data were mixed but generally
point to a global economy that is
chugging along just fine amid heightened
macro policy uncertainty.
Terms of analytical nuance, the November
ISM economy weighted composite PMI data
supported our outlook for accelerating
above consensus growth. The November ISM
services PMI data supported our
resilient US economy theme. The November
ISM economy weighted composite PMI data
supported our resilient US economy theme
as well. And then the ISM services PMI
data supported the our tariff pig and
the inflation python thesis and suggests
we may be approaching the end of that
process.
The weak negative impulse in November
auto sales data supported our U-shaped
economy thesis. The strong negative
impulses in the September industrial
production and capacity utilization data
supported our U-shaped economy thesis
and the strong negative impulse in the
November ADP private payrolls data
supported our U-shaped economy thesis
and suggest the Fed is falling further
behind the rates curve. The sharp
deceleration in median annual pay for
job changers in the ADP survey at 6.3%
year-over-year, which was the lowest
since January of 2021, supports our
U-shaped economy thesis and also
suggests the Fed is falling further
behind the rates curve. The 10ear
nominal Treasury yield is at roughly the
same level as the Fed funds rate. Rarely
in history is this dynamic sustained.
Historically, the 10ear is roughly 120
basis points wider than the Fed funds
rate. And the current spread implies the
10ear should be either greater than 5%
or the Fed funds rate should be at or
below 3%. Somebody's wrong there. We're
guessing it's probably the cabal of
unelected economists as opposed to the
hundreds of millions of market
participants around the world. Uh
according to the November final
composite PMI data, Europe and Japan are
outperforming other key economies in
growth momentum terms. Lastly, consensus
shortrun potential real GDP growth
estimates are trending higher in the US
and in the China and the US, sideways in
Japan, and lower in the Euro zone and
UK. Consensus shortrun potential real
GDP growth estimates are also trending
higher in India, sideways in Brazil, and
lower in Australia, Canada, and
Switzerland. And then consensus shortrun
potential headline CBI estimates are
trending higher in the UK and US,
sideways in Japan, and lower in China
and the Euro zone. And then finally,
consensus shortrun potential headlines,
CBI estimates are trending higher in
Australia and Brazil and lower in
Canada, India, and Switzerland. And for
those who may not be familiar, when we
say consensus shortrun potential rail
GDP or headline CBI estimates, that's
just taking the blend of the one-year
forward and two-year forward u estimate
to get a general sense of the general
growth rate that the uh Wall Street
consensus is sort of applying to each of
these economies. that removes the impact
of of base effects uh from that from
that um from that calculus to get a
sense hey are people just generally
revising up their growth expectations
because there's obviously a good policy
mix uh in support of growth so
supportive demographic and productivity
dynamics or vice versa and same
obviously with inflation and for the
drivers of inflation so um so obviously
trending higher is good for growth
trending higher is bad for inflation and
vice versa so uh as always members of
our global investor community can trust
that our institutional grade risk
management overlays kiss and Dr. Mo will
help our portfolios navigate these
emergent risks better than investors
attempting to manage risk without
systematic rules or relying on
fundamental research alone.
Transitioning to our 42 micro dashboard
here as always wrap up with a question
from our community. This one's titled
small businesses in the fourth turning
says hello team 42 and community. I've
been a subscriber for about a month and
I'm excited to utilize KISS to manage
the larger majority of my liquid net
worth. I've recently come across an
opportunity to buy a small business with
steady cash flow in the home services
sector. I am excited about the
opportunity to purchase this small
business. However, I'm beginning to
wonder what the implications before
turning will be on small businesses as
the for turning progresses. What types
of impacts should we expect to see in
small businesses across uh America? So,
uh excellent question. I'll start by
saying um it's hard to ascertain that
answer without you know applying some
sort of sectoral specific overlay
because obviously there's a lot going in
the economy particularly as it relates
to the sectors that are going to be uh
more or less impacted by AI there's
going to be a lot of dispersion uh um in
the coming you know let's call it 5 to
10 years in that regard so uh
tableabling that uh the one thing I will
say is one thing that's consistent
across fourth turnings that we
identified in our investing during our
fourth turning research which was an
empirical deep dive study that we
performed in the summer for 2023. One of
the things I figured out a lot of
things, but one of the more important
key takeaways from that study uh was you
tend to see explosive growth in the size
of sovereign just general explosive
growth in the size of government and
government borrowing. And as a function
of that, taking this back to small
business, what's likely to happen is
that we see an increasing crowding out
um as long as the Fed is not monetizing
uh debt uh at a rate uh that uh that
allows for that capital for that
crowding out impact to to toilate. um
this is exactly what we're seeing here
and it's contributing to the K-shaped
economy. So uh if you think about uh you
know throw a few statistics at you uh if
you think about the approximate next 12
month marketable treasury debt uh uh um
supply that we've seen uh uh in terms of
the the time series there uh that
statistic which is now well north of 11
trillion up from about three you know
three four trillion prior to the
pandemic that statistic is now gobbling
up about 36% of global savings up from a
long run mean of about 22%. That same
statistic is gobbling up about 215ish%
of US savings up from a long run mean of
about 120%. So the delta between the
current amount of savings that Uncle Sam
is essentially gobbling up relative to
uh the long run means of those time
series suggests that that is the kind of
amount of crowding out those savings
instead of being you know going to
capitalize small businesses going to
capitalize mortgages going to capitalize
other things that are sort of being left
behind in this K-shaped economy. they're
not going those places and they're
essentially going to capitalize the
explosive growth of the public sector
balance sheet. And so it's essentially
like the economy is having a stroke if
you will in the sense that the money
which used to go to support small
businesses and other uh private sector
economic activity is no longer doing so.
Uh so if we assume that you know we're
not going to this this this sort of
explosive growth of the fiscal uh uh
balance sheet the the explosive growth
of the public sector balance sheet is
unlikely to uh end until we're on the
other side of the for turning because
obviously it's probably going to
accelerate from here as we get approach
the climax phase of the for turning. If
that is true um and obviously we believe
it is true. If that is true then it's
unlikely this crowding out impact uh is
going to ever really truly be you know
fixed or solved. It might go eb and
flow, you know, particularly next year
when we start to see the impact of what
we've been calling for, which is
structural regime change at the Federal
Reserve. If we start to see structural
regime change at the Federal Reserve,
which a lot made monetary policy a
little bit more supportive of diverting
uh of sort of not diverting, but really
just um you know, amilarating the impact
of that explosive growth of public
sector borrowing. If you can sort of
make that digestion process a little bit
easier from a monetary policy
standpoint, then you would ultimately
have a little bit more trickle down in
terms of the capital flows that uh could
go to capitalize small businesses, the
mortgage market, etc. So, that's
something we're expecting that's part of
that's been part of our paradigm C uh uh
view since we offered the paradigm C uh
theme back in um mid late uh April. Um
so, you know, obviously we've enjoyed
this tremendous uh 42 macro paradigm C
bull market uh since then. So, u we'll
wrap it there. Derry still here
presenting our macro minute for
Wednesday, December 3rd, 2025. Best of
luck out there today. We'll catch you
